chunk_summary: Stepwise evaluation of persistent ALT elevation in children with obesity focuses on confirming MASLD, excluding alternative etiologies, and determining referral needs based on severity and red flags.
learning_objectives: Understand the structured approach to evaluating persistent ALT elevation in pediatric obesity.
Identify first-line tests for excluding alternative causes of elevated ALT.
Recognize red flags requiring referral to pediatric gastroenterology/hepatology.
key_facts: Persistent ALT elevation supports MASLD diagnosis after excluding other causes.
Step 1: Repeat ALT with AST and GGT after 3–6 months of lifestyle intensification.
Step 2: First-line tests include hepatitis B/C markers, tissue transglutaminase IgA, autoimmune markers, and ceruloplasmin in older children.
Step 3: Ultrasound can support steatosis but does not exclude MASLD; refer for red flags like jaundice or hepatosplenomegaly.
Elevated ALT with systemic symptoms warrants checking bilirubin, albumin, and INR.
reference_quote: Persistently elevated ALT over repeated measurements supports a diagnosis on the metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) spectrum when other causes are excluded.
document_key_topics: Persistent ALT elevation in pediatric obesity
Referral criteria for severe liver disease
